claim: HY OAS-to-VIX ratio is the single best real-time indicator of credit-equity stress transmission.
reason: Full-sample evidence shows only 36% hit rate (4/11) for HY threshold producing equity drawdowns. Lacks backtesting. Specific thresholds (15, 25) are post-hoc calibration. Sensitive to VIX methodology and HY composition changes. A heuristic at best, not the single best indicator.

claim: Shadow term premium is a quantifiable concept that captures private credit's hidden contribution to the cost of capital.
reason: Unfalsifiable by design — the opacity is the problem. Author's own caveat: mark-to-recovery gap could be 5% or 25%. A worry dressed in term-premium terminology that adds false precision to a valid underlying concern.

claim: Mainstream structural models (Merton, DSGE) all fail to capture credit-equity linkage dynamics.
reason: Category error: Merton models are not used for macro regime forecasting. BGG and Curdia-Woodford partially address endogenous credit within NK framework. SFC/Minsky models remain computationally impractical and lack real-time forecasting track records. The critique has theoretical merit but the sweeping dismissal does not.

claim: The direction of credit-equity contagion has reversed relative to pre-GFC episodes.
reason: The mechanisms have evolved (CLO plumbing, warehouse lines, fund financing vs MBS), but the direction of transmission (credit stress → equity impact) remains the same. Framing a change in pipes as a change in flow direction is misleading and could cause practitioners to dismiss valid historical patterns.

claim: Equity options contain more accurate credit information than credit spreads themselves.
reason: Conflates speed of repricing with accuracy of signal. Equity-specific factors (buybacks, index rebalancing, options market-making) add noise. A fast-repricing but noisy instrument is not necessarily superior to a slower but structurally informed one (CLO managers with bottom-up analysis). No empirical validation provided.

claim: Credit-to-equity causal primacy is universal across all types of economic downturns.
reason: Fails the 2020 test (simultaneous exogenous shock), 2022 test (rate-driven equity duration hit), and dot-com episode (equity bubble collapsed first, credit stable 18 months). Only the weaker claim — credit-to-equity primacy holds in credit-cycle-driven downturns — survives.

claim: Specific HY OAS thresholds (500bp, 550-650bp, 600bp) are precise point estimates at which credit stress becomes self-reinforcing.
reason: All specific numbers carry false precision from samples of 4-6 heterogeneous episodes. Market structure changes (CLOs, ETFs, private credit) mean historical thresholds are not directly transferable. Only the directional claim that a regime-dependent threshold zone of ~450-600bp exists survived.

claim: Faster post-GFC contagion resolution means lower net risk for medium-term investors.
reason: The temporal compression is real for non-recessionary episodes (6-7 months vs 10-12 months post-GFC), but the argument excludes 2001-02 and 2007-09 as genuine recessions — precisely the scenarios where credit-equity contagion matters most. A framework that shows crises are less dangerous after excluding the dangerous crises is circular.
